cards.front: How big should an emergency fund be?
cards.back: Usually 3-6 months of expenses, instantly accessible.
Compound interest earns interest on interest — early years compound most.
Spreading across assets limits damage if one crashes.
Usually clear high-interest debt first — its rate beats most returns.
Auto-save on payday before you can spend it.
~50% needs, 30% wants, 20% savings/debt — a starter budget.

cards.front: Why do fees matter so much?
cards.back: They compound against you; 1%/yr can eat a large chunk over decades.

cards.front: What does inflation do to cash?
cards.back: Quietly erodes purchasing power — idle cash loses real value.
Spending rising with income; bank part of each raise.

cards.front: Key to a good credit score?
cards.back: Pay on time and keep balances low vs your limit.
72 ÷ annual return ≈ years to double your money (e.g. 8% → ~9 years).
Investing a fixed amount on a schedule, smoothing out price swings.

cards.front: How to match risk to time?
cards.back: Money needed soon → safe; money for years away → can take more risk.

cards.front: Timing the market vs time in market?
cards.back: Time in the market usually beats timing it — staying invested wins long-term.
Net worth (assets − debts) shows real progress; income alone can mislead.
Every dollar/hour spent here can't be used there — weigh the next-best option.
Debt that builds value/income can pay off; high-interest consumer debt rarely does.
Big, rare, unaffordable risks — not small costs you could pay yourself.
Saving a bit monthly for known future costs (car, gifts) so they don't shock you.
Use a 24-hour (or longer) wait before big non-essential buys.
Autopay avoids late fees and credit damage; just keep a buffer.
Tax-deferred or tax-free growth compounds faster than a taxable account.
Locked-up money forces bad choices in emergencies; keep a cash cushion.
